Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: True Yoga Pte Ltd
Party_Details.Law_Firm: Audent Chambers LLC / LVM Law Chambers LLC

Facts.Text: Patrick John Wee Ewe Seng signed an employment contract with True Yoga Pte Ltd
CJ Group Pte Ltd transferred its shares in the plaintiffs to True Fitness Holdings (Singapore) Pte Ltd
Tongfang Kontafarma Holdings Limited acquired majority shareholding in True Group (Singapore)

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the defendant was legally responsible for mismanaging the closures of True Group (Malaysia) and True Group (Thailand)
Party_Details.Rule: A director must act in the best interests of the company and manage its affairs diligently
Party_Details.Application: The defendant breached his fiduciary and contractual duties by grossly mismanaging the closures of the regional businesses, continuing to sell long-term memberships despite impending closures, and severely damaging the company's reputation.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the defendant breached his duties by failing to properly manage the closures, which damaged the reputation of the True Group.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Wee Ewe Seng Patrick John

Facts.Text: Patrick John Wee Ewe Seng became the Group CEO of True Group
Patrick John Wee Ewe Seng was terminated from his position as Group CEO
In February 2017, Patrick John Wee Ewe Seng knew that True Group (Malaysia) was facing grave financial difficulties and impending closure
Patrick John Wee Ewe Seng caused S$3.25 million to be paid to his beneficially owned company
In March 2017, Patrick John Wee Ewe Seng told Evolution Wellness that True Group had decided to exit both the Malaysian and Thailand markets

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the defendant was legally responsible for the financial mismanagement leading to the closures of True Group (Malaysia) and True Group (Thailand)
Party_Details.Rule: A director is required to manage company affairs in a manner that serves the best interests of the company and its stakeholders
Party_Details.Application: The closures of the Malaysian and Thai businesses were necessary commercial decisions due to grave financial difficulties, and the cessation of operations was managed reasonably under the circumstances.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court concluded that the defendant's actions were not in the best interests of the company, leading to reputational damage and financial loss.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Wee Ewe Seng Patrick John

Facts.Text: Patrick John Wee Ewe Seng was terminated from his position as Group CEO
Unpaid salary of $120,000 per month for the period of 1 January 2018 to 30 April 2018
Tongfang sent a letter requesting Patrick John Wee Ewe Seng to resign from his position as CEO
Patrick John Wee Ewe Seng's employment contract was impliedly extended beyond 12 March 2018

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the defendant was disputed terminated and entitled to unpaid salary
Party_Details.Rule: An employee is entitled to compensation if terminated without cause before the end of the contract term
Party_Details.Application: The termination of employment was without valid cause, thereby entitling the recovery of full unpaid salary arrears and twelve months' salary in lieu of notice.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the termination was justified for cause due to the defendant's mismanagement, dismissing the claim for 12 months' salary in lieu of notice. However, the court allowed the claim for the actual unpaid salary accrued prior to the termination date at a reduced rate.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: True Yoga Pte Ltd
Party_Details.Law_Firm: Audent Chambers LLC / LVM Law Chambers LLC

Facts.Text: True Yoga Pte Ltd terminated Patrick John Wee Ewe Seng's employment

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the company was legally responsible for disputed termination and unpaid salary claims
Party_Details.Rule: A company may terminate an employee for cause if there is evidence of misconduct or negligence
Party_Details.Application: The termination was entirely justified for cause due to the former CEO's gross mismanagement and breach of duties, thereby legally disentitling him to any salary in lieu of notice.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court determined that the termination was for cause, negating the 12-month notice claim, but held the company liable to pay the actual unpaid salary arrears of $62,660.70 accrued prior to the termination date.
